title: AI Dev 26 x SF | Ashwyn Sharma: Every App Needs a Voice UI. Here's How to Build It
transcript: I'm Ashin Sharma, CEO and co-founder of Vocal Bridge. Super excited to be talking about voice interfaces and how you can build them yourself with Vocal Bridge, a company I co-ounded with Andrew Inc's AI fund and deep learning.AI. Personally, I've spent the last decade of my life shipping voice AI at scale at companies like Meta, Salesforce, and Auto.AI. AI and I've experienced the pain behind building products uh that involve speech and voice firstand. So, you know, here we go. If you've ever dabbled in uh building voice agents and voice AI and speech products, this might, you know, seem like very familiar to you. Um what usually starts with a simple, hey, you know, I want to build voice AI. Um maybe I'll start with speechto text and text to speech. maybe I'll also sandwich uh an LLM between those two components and that should be really it right and then you start working on shipping that uh to production and then couple of weeks in you're really you know running into obscure terms like voice activity detection uh procity and endpointing and you know baron handling turn detection it really grows into um this hot mess this alphabet soup and um you know you what starts off as like a couple couple of weeks uh worth of project work uh ends up with you know 6 months sometimes uh years of uh you know uh uh time it takes to uh for the team to deploy uh the product in production. So what if I told you I could you know make this entire stack disappear with a snap. So presenting vocab one-stop shop for voice AI a fully managed voice AI platform. More specifically today, I'm going to be introducing you to three surfaces, three interfaces, three ways you can plug in Vocal Bridge into your stack. Number one, applications. So you can add voice to your existing applications or you can build voice apps from scratch using Vocal Bridge. Number two, you can add voice to AI agents. So give voice to your existing AI agent. Number three, I'm specifically very excited about this one, which is using voice as a tool. So, you know, if you want to build AI agents that are truly multimodal, then you need to give those AI agents the ability to switch modality as they see fit, depending on the use case and the task at hand. So, we'll start. I I'll be doing three demos, three live demos. So, I've I've been praying to the demo gods for a week now. Uh so, here we go. The first demo is going to be me using voice to interact with a very dynamic Reactbased UI. and I'm going to be playing a game uh of tic-tac-toe. Here we go. >> Hey, I'm your stage co-pilot. Ask me anything and I'll draw the >> Hey there. Ready to jump in? >> What do you want? >> Let's play tic-tac-toe. >> Your X, your move. >> I will take the center one, please. corner. Your move. >> I will take top right. >> Top center. Let's go. Your move. Okay, you got me. Nicely played. >> All right, there you go. So, hopefully you could appreciate the fact that the voice agent was truly embedded in the application. It had complete contextual awareness of the state of the game and how I was interacting with the board. So, you know, you saw that I was able to use my voice to place markers on the board. But then when I used my mouse or I could have used my keyboard, the voice agent completely understood what had happened, right? It it responded by saying, you know, add one and and well done, right? So, how can you do this yourself? How can you build a voice user interface yourself? So, three easy steps. Well, actually four easy steps. Uh step zero is mpm install vocal bridge vocalbridge AI uh SDK react that'll um uh that'll ensure that you have our um SDK and react components on hand. So once you've done that you can wrap your application with our vocal bridge provider and then essentially you are pointing it at a serverside API that can mint vocal bridge tokens using the vocal bridge API key and that's pretty straightforward. You can head to our dashboard and create an API key yourself. That's all you need to get started. Step number two. So this is really the critical one, right? This is how the voice agent is able to talk to your application and your application is talk is able to talk back to the voice agent. So uh React SDK gives you two hooks. Hook number one is on action and hook number two is send action. So looking back at the at the tic-tac-toe example, um when the voice agent wanted to place a mark on the board, it sent a client action called place mark with a tpple of row and column, right? And that tpple was sent to the application. The application ended up showing that that mark on the board. So all you had to do as a developer is use the onaction hook and then handle the client action however you want to. Step number two or the second aspect of this birection communication between the voice agent and the application is is the user place mark action. So you saw how I placed a mark with my mouse on the board. The uh the application sent an event um uh a client action back to the voice agent with user place mark and the tpple and the state of the board so that the voice agent understands how the game is going. And then finally in order for you to configure these client actions that are birectional all you have to do is use this declarative format which is a JSON. So in this JSON uh the first client action is base mark the direction is agent to app and then the description field is a natural language description of what the client action is and the payload that the agent should be sending the application. So in this case it was just a row column tuple. The second client action is user place mark. the direction is reversed. So app to agent the application is sending a client action back to the agent and then the behavior is respond. So on vocal bridge you basically get two different types of behaviors when the application is sending uh a client action back to the agent. So one is respond that means that the voice agent is going to respond to that incoming client action right away and acknowledge it. So in the in the tic-tac-toe example, you saw how I placed the mark. That was the winning mark and then the voice agent immediately reacted to it. But there are circumstances where you want the voice agent to just ingest that event into the context window and then just use that information for later. So it won't immediately respond to it verbally. And then you can also install our CL CLI. So pip install local bridge. And then you can use the command line to set the set and configure the prompt and the client actions and do everything else that you can do on the UI. And this is the second surface. So giving voice to your AI agents. What you see on the right is a typical LM agent, right? In this case, backed by Claude and it's completely text based. It's a chatbased interface. So I can chat with it say hey. And it responded back. But what what if I told you with just a few lines of code actually technically it's just like a single line if you if you condense it all but with just a couple of lines of code you can um really give voice add to this specific agent with just a click of a button >> and just like that I have a voice saying Claude on the back end. What do you want to ask? >> Hey can you explain uh how light years work as a unit of distance? Let me look that up. Here's the answer. A lightyear is a distance light travels in one year through space about 5.88 trillion miles. Astronomers use it because space is so vast that miles and kilome get unwieldy and despite the name it's a unit of >> So what is the distance between Mountain View and San Francisco in light years? on it searching. The two cities are about 30 m apart, which is roughly 0.00005 lightyear, about 5 billion of a lightyear. It's such a tiny fraction because a lightyear is so vast that it makes everyday distances almost meaningless in that measure. >> Well, that's great. Thank you so much. I am sure this crowd at AI Dev appreciates your help. >> You're very welcome. Glad to help show off what we can do. Anything else you'd like to ask or explore? Nope, that's it. Thank you. >> My pleasure. Thanks for chatting and enjoy the rest of the event. Goodbye for now. >> All right. So, hopefully you were able to appreciate how the Vocal Bridge agent, the voice agent, had a brain of its own. It was not just doing speech to text and text to speech and sandwiching your LLM in between. It had a brain of its own. It knew when to delegate a query to your AI agent versus just handling small talk and conversation by itself. And it's really important because if you try to use the traditional uh if you go the traditional route which is cascading a speech to text model then LM the LM or your AI agent and then text to speech then the onus of maintaining the conversation and managing the state of the dialogue uh falls on your LM and you don't want to do that because that crowds the context window of your LM and you don't want the LM to be distracted from the task it's it's supposed to be carrying out. So again you guessed it right. We make it really easy for you to do this. Use our command line um interface VBcon set AI agent enable true and then give it a natural language description so that the voice agent knows when to delegate a query to uh to your agent versus handling it by itself. And then on the client side you can leverage uh the use AI agent hook that we offer as part of our SDK. And then um all you have to do is um use the onquery call back and point it to your LM's endpoint or your agent's endpoint. Once your agent and your or your LM responds back uh with the text, it forwards it back to the vocal bridge agent and then vocal bridge decides how to communicate it back to the user conversation. And finally, this is my favorite one using voice as a tool. So in this case I again have the chatbas interface on the left and uh it's asking me how the demo is going. I will say going great so far need need help brainstorm some ideas on what to do next after the demo. All right. So, it's thinking it decides that it says, "Honestly, this is faster than voice. Want me to spin up a quick voice session?" So, it decided, you know, brainstorming is easier with voice. Lowers the cognitive load. It's a more natural interface. I'm going to say, "Sure." >> Brainstorm mode. What's on your mind? >> Hey, I'm trying to decide what to do after this demo. >> Got it. Are you up for something deep and technical or would you rather coast and just soak up ideas? >> I don't mind uh going deep and technical. >> In that case, Tony Lur's talk at 405 on why agents forget sounds like a fit. Enough time to grab a coffee and maybe swing by a booth. Sounds doable to me. >> Okay, sounds good. Let's do it. >> Great. You've got a plan. Start with Tony at 4:05, then ease into Andrew session at 5:00. Enjoy the talks and I'll see you at happy hour. >> Thank you. And >> all right. It was able to do that by itself. Really handled the context well and um yeah, again you guessed it right. You can do it yourself. You can build it yourself. Um use standard LM2 uh schema and in this case make phone call. The input schema is just the phone number, the name and the purpose. purpose is more like a prompt um in addition to the base prompt for the for the voice agent and vocal bridge once once the LM decides to make the tool call um you know it's really calling our um our command line interface underneath so we make it really easy VB call with a phone number and the name of the person and the JSON has the purpose and any additional context you want the voice to hide handle and then finally don't forget to enable uh out outbound calling and accept set the terms of service. Uh make sure that you're using it for compliant purposes only. And those were all the demos. So before I wrap up, um this is what I want you to walk away with our vision.
